## 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Methodology
24 April 2025, 12:00pm
2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Methodology presents background information, operational and methodological details, and key results from Aotearoa New Zealand’s 2023 Post-enumeration Survey (PES).
15 May 2025 : We have corrected values in Table 2 for MELAA ethnicity, and updated percent values in Table 6 rows 4 to 7 (Individual forms to Admin enumerations) to use the PES estimated population as the denominator.
Read this paper online or download the PDF below.
- 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Methodology – corrected Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 1.5 MB

### 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Methodology – corrected
Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 1.5 MB
Introduction Survey implementation Methodology Results Glossary

### 1.1 Summary and contents
2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Methodology presents background information, operational and methodological details, and key results from Aotearoa New Zealand’s 2023 Post-enumeration Survey (PES).
The 2023 PES was carried out soon after the 2023 Census of Population and Dwellings (held on 7 March 2023), with interviewers visiting 16,970 private dwellings throughout New Zealand.
The main purpose of the PES was to gather information on the completeness of census coverage, that is, to estimate how many New Zealand residents were missed by the census, counted more than once, or counted in error.
PES results provide the official measures of person response and coverage (undercount, overcount, and net coverage) for each census. These measures are used as key performance indicators (KPIs) for the relevant census and provide a critical input for producing accurate population estimates and projections following the census.
This report is organised into four chapters:
- Introduction – provides some of the key results from the 2023 PES and an outline of what the PES is and why it is carried out.
- Survey implementation – covers the scoping and objectives of the survey, sample design, and field collection processes.
- Methodology – describes processes such as linking, treatment of residents temporarily overseas, late census returns, misclassification, and estimation.
- Results – presents the 2023 PES KPIs, 2023 Census KPIs measured by PES, and coverage results for various subpopulations.
Coverage results from the 2023 PES showed that:
- the 2023 Census counted 98.9 percent of people usually resident and present in New Zealand on census night, with a credible interval of 98.6–99.1 percent
- the national median estimated net undercount was 1.1 percent or 57,200 people
- the 2023 national net undercount rate of 1.1 percent was the lowest since coverage measurement began in 1996, and was down from the 2.6 percent recorded in the 2018 Census
- there were 116,000 people not counted at all (gross undercount), and 58,800 people who were counted more than once or in error (gross overcount)
- people of Māori ethnicity had a net undercount of 1.6 percent (14,400 people), compared with 1.6 percent for Pacific peoples (7,100 people), and 2.2 percent for the Asian ethnic group (19,500 people)
- people of Māori descent had a net undercount of 1.5 percent in 2023, down from 4.2 percent in 2018
- young adults (15–29 years) have traditionally had the highest net undercount by age group, but in 2023, it was 1.4 percent, down from 4.7 percent in 2018 – the largest decrease across all age groups in 2023
- the 0–14, 15–29, and 30–44-year age groups had similar rates of net undercount in 2023, within the credible intervals of each other
- males had a higher net undercount (1.5 percent) than females (0.8 percent) or people of another gender (0.6 percent), although people of another gender had the highest level of uncertainty (credible interval: -2.4–3.8 percent) (data on gender was collected in the PES for the first time in 2023)
- people born overseas had a higher net undercount (2.0 percent) than people born in New Zealand (0.8 percent)
- the North Island had a higher net undercount (1.2 percent) than the South Island (1.0 percent), due largely to the impact of the higher undercount in the Auckland region
- the Auckland region, home to 33 percent of the population, accounted for approximately 42 percent of the net undercount
- across the 16 regions of New Zealand, the net undercount ranged from 0.5 percent in Hawke’s Bay to 1.4 percent in Auckland, with Hawke’s Bay showing the highest level of uncertainty (credible interval: -0.7–1.8 percent).
As with all data estimated from a survey with a small sample size, median estimates provided by PES are subject to uncertainties. The true value, however, is expected to lie within a range close to the median. This range is referred to as the credible interval. For more details on credible intervals and the estimation process, please refer to Methodology .
Methodological changes between PES instances also mean that comparisons across time should be made with caution. This is especially true from the 2018 PES onwards, due to changes in census methodology described in Using a combined census model for the 2023 Census , as well as in the definition of a census response.
Stats NZ, like other national statistical organisations, makes a concerted effort to achieve the highest possible coverage in the census.
The PES is a household survey undertaken shortly after the census to evaluate the completeness of census coverage of people. It involves an independent re-capture of a statistically designed sample of private dwellings and the people within them covered by the national census. PES responses are linked to census records – both census form responses and admin enumerations (see Using a combined census model for the 2023 Census ) – to identify instances of miscount, such as people who were missed by the census, counted more than once, or counted in error. The PES sample is then adjusted to reflect the national population using statistical models and estimation methods.
Every census inevitably has some level of miscount, arising from a range of factors. For example, people might be missed (undercount) due to:
- not receiving census materials at their address
- deliberate avoidance of the census
- people being reluctant to open their door to strangers
- shifting from one house to another during the census collection period
- multiple households living at the same address
- people being away temporarily (for example, work or school)
- people having no usual residence (for example, homeless people)
- young children’s parents not completing a form on their behalf
- recent migrants not being familiar with their obligations
- dwellings being entirely missed by census collectors
- occupied dwellings being misclassified as vacant.
On the other hand, people might be counted more than once or counted in error (overcount) due to:
- filling out forms at multiple addresses
- filling out a form when they were not in New Zealand on census night
- filling out a form while living at school or university, and also being counted at the home of their parents
- inclusion as an admin enumeration, either in addition to a census response (hence duplication) or when they were not in the target population (erroneous inclusion)
- joint custody arrangements where both custodial parents complete a form for the same child
- shifting from one house to another during the census collection period and completing forms at both addresses
- living in institutions, as it is common for people to be enumerated both in an institution and elsewhere, especially when tenure in the institution is short
- erroneous inclusion of people who have died
- being born after census night (newborn babies)
- residents completing a form despite being temporarily overseas on census night
- departing overseas in the lead-up to census and completing a form prior to departure
- forms being completed for family pets that were not picked up during census processing.
A more detailed discussion of other traditional types of census miscount can be found in Post-enumeration survey 2018: Methods and results .
The use of administrative data in the 2018 and 2023 Censuses to add individuals missed through traditional census field enumeration has successfully reduced the undercount. However, this change has also introduced additional sources of overcount. For example, some individuals appear in the census file as both an individual response and an admin enumeration. The 2023 Census aimed to minimise the impact of this on the final coverage rate, though it can never be fully eliminated – see Linking 2023 Census responses to the Integrated Data Infrastructure .
Since 1996, Stats NZ has used the PES to estimate the amount of miscount in the national census. Before this, Stats NZ evaluated the general quality of census data, but did not directly measure the level of undercount or overcount through a post-censal survey. Shifting to a PES brought New Zealand in line with international best practice for countries that carry out a full-field enumeration census. For example, Australia, South Africa, Zambia, Rwanda, England and Wales, Scotland, and the United States of America all conduct separate post-censal surveys that involve re-counting a statistically structured sample of dwellings and the people living in them. According to the Report on the results of the UNSD survey on 2020 round population and housing census ,  99 of 158 responding countries (63 percent) conducted a PES to measure the quality of census data.
In New Zealand, the key outputs produced by the PES include the net census undercount, gross undercount, gross overcount, and the PES estimated population. These outputs are produced at the national level and for a range of subpopulations.
Census coverage rates are a critical input into official population estimates (known as the estimated resident population or ERP), which are the basis for demographic projections and indexes. These estimates and projections are used widely throughout the New Zealand data ecosystem, including by:
- central and local government, to assess current and future needs for facilities and services in health, education, and social welfare, as well as in the allocation of public funds to provide these services
- businesses and community organisations, for planning, research, and marketing purposes
- Stats NZ, as population denominators for a range of statistics, and as population benchmarks in calibration and estimation procedures for social surveys.
The PES also provides official measurement of census response rates – the proportion of the population and subpopulations who completed and returned census forms. Coverage and response rate measures allow Stats NZ to assess the effectiveness of the current census through KPIs and to inform planning for future censuses.

## 2    Survey implementation
The PES survey sample is designed to produce estimates of net census undercount, gross undercount, and gross overcount for the following key population groups:
- usually resident population in Aotearoa New Zealand on census night
- gender (male, female, people of another gender)
- age (0–14 years, 15–29 years, 30–44 years, 45–64 years, 65–74 years, 75+ years)
- Māori descent (electoral) population*
- ethnicity (level 1 groups)
- birthplace (New Zealand-born, overseas-born)
- regional council areas
- territorial authorities and Auckland local board areas.
*The PES uses the electoral concept of Māori descent, which is used to determine electoral populations. This differs from the census concept, which allows respondents to state that they don’t know if they were descended from Māori. For further details about the concept and its uses refer to Māori descent electoral – 2023 Census: Information by concept .
The PES is not designed to:
- locate people who intentionally avoid being found
- use other methods, like administrative records, to find missing people
- measure or produce estimates of dwelling coverage*
- address classification difficulties with dwellings (such as what is a private dwelling) where counting, or not, of a dwelling could have an impact on the results and could result in people being systematically missed or double-counted, by census or PES
- address the effect of differences in enumeration between the census and the PES.
*Measurement of under- and over-coverage in the dwelling counts produced by the census. Under-coverage represents the omission of dwellings belonging to the target population, while over-coverage represents the inclusion of dwellings that do not belong to the target population or are included more than once. Ensuring the PES used a high-quality dwelling frame was a key priority in 2023. However, the measurement of dwelling coverage was excluded due to the limitations identified in the 2018 PES and the move towards an estimation approach focused on person coverage.
The scope of a survey is determined by how the population is defined. The 2023 PES uses the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) definitions for target and survey populations:
“A target population is the population outlined in the survey objects about which information is to be sought and a survey population is the population from which information can be obtained in the survey.” ( OECD Glossary of Statistical Terms ).
The PES incorporates census target populations (all people and dwellings in New Zealand) and census key performance indicators (KPIs) into its target population definitions, making it unique when compared with standard social surveys. To measure coverage in the census, target population definitions for the PES are broader than for census. The PES definitions include people who should have been counted but were not and people who were counted but should not have been.

### 2.3 Population definitions
The population definitions for 2023 PES remain the same as those used in 2018. Information in this section has been provided for context, for detailed definitions refer to the 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation .
The target population for dwellings is all private dwellings that should have been or were counted by the 2023 Census.
The survey population for dwellings is all private dwellings on the North Island, South Island, and Waiheke Island, with practical inclusions and exclusions.  For ease of survey data collection, the 2023 PES also includes permanent private and non-private dwellings (NPDs) in the survey population. Private dwellings can include emergency and transitional housing provided by the government. NPDs are included in enumeration to find private dwellings associated with a non-private dwelling, for example, on-site staff accommodation. However, any NPDs are excluded from interview and estimation.
Additional dwelling types, classified as ‘other’ private dwellings, are excluded from survey data collection. These include mobile dwellings and temporary or improvised dwellings, which are generally excluded due to the high likelihood that their location will change throughout the census and PES operations. However, mobile and under-construction dwellings are included if they are fixed to one location and have permanent private residents.
For further information, refer to 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation .
The target population for people is all usually resident people in New Zealand who should have been or were counted by the census on census night.
The 2023 PES survey population for people consists of individuals usually residing in New Zealand in private dwellings or staying at one during the PES survey period (including both New Zealand residents and overseas visitors). This excludes people living in dwellings that do not meet the dwelling definition in the previous section. It also excludes:
- people who died after census night
- overseas diplomats, their families, and people living with them.
Two special cases are included because of their contribution to over-coverage:
- overseas visitors as they may have been counted in the census as usual residents
- babies born after census night as they may have been enumerated in the census.
For further information refer to the 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation .

### 2.4 Operational design and planning
This section outlines the intended design for survey activities and associated sampling. It is presented separately from the implementation details to highlight where changes were made during the survey operation.

#### 2.4.1 Sampling for enumeration
The 2023 PES sample design, like the previous instances, was purpose-built to balance the desired accuracy targets with cost and sample size constraints. The sample design for 2023 PES was developed for a Bayesian estimation framework.
The first stage of sample selection involved selecting 1,500 primary sampling units (PSUs) from the 2018 Household Survey Frame (HSF). The HSF is the standard sampling frame Stats NZ uses to select samples and manage overlap between household surveys. The 2018 HSF consisted of 52,973 meshblocks, grouped into 23,174 PSUs, which exclude offshore meshblocks. Each PSU in the 2018 HSF has attributes determined by census data. After selecting PSUs, addresses corresponding to private dwellings within each PSU were selected.
The 2023 Census used the same data sources for addresses, despite not using the HSF. To mitigate the risk of dependency, the PES carried out enumeration independent of the census. Staffing and timing of fieldwork were managed to avoid any overlap between the census and PES operations. For further information about the sourcing of addresses and mitigation of dependency, refer to the 2023 Post-enumeration survey: Standard design for coverage estimation .
Additional considerations were made to improve the sampling efficiency and minimise overlap with other household surveys when selecting PSUs. The PSUs were grouped into strata. These strata groups were created from a combination of geographic attributes, included to manage cost of operations, and concepts related to the survey objectives derived from demographic variables, for example, the potential to be missed or miscounted by the census. The sample design does not include any explicit over-sampling of demographic groups but groups more likely to have under-coverage are more likely to be over-sampled because of the strata design.
The sample design grouped PSUs into 102 strata based on:
- 12 broad geographic regions (Gisborne and Hawke’s Bay regional council areas are combined, as are the West Coast, Marlborough, Nelson, and Tasman)
- a mix of urban and rural
- the census ‘stream’, as outlined in the Collection model for the 2023 Census
- socio-economic characteristics captured in the NZ Deprivation index.
Territorial authority and Auckland local board areas (TALB) were not explicitly included in the strata due to sample size constraints. However, the PSU selection process was designed to implicitly account for TALBs and maximise the probability of selecting at least one PSU in each TALB.
As with other Stats NZ household surveys, the 2023 PES PSU selection used controls to minimise the sample overlap with other surveys. This is done to reduce respondent burden by minimising the number of people selected to take part in more than one survey. This methodology allows us to share the household frame with other government departments and minimise overlap across organisations.

#### 2.4.2 Desktop canvassing and field enumeration
Using the selected sample PSUs for enumeration, PES conducted a dwelling enumeration exercise to identify dwellings eligible for interview within each PSU. Enumeration consisted of two phases: desktop canvassing and field enumeration. The aim of both phases was to identify all private dwellings in a designated area.
The aim of desktop canvassing was to enumerate the 75 percent of PSUs expected to be the most challenging and add more contextual information for field staff in the next phase of work.  Staff used a range of publicly available resources (such as satellite imagery, rates databases, and real estate listings) to investigate addresses and record relevant information.
The aim of field enumeration was for field staff to investigate all dwellings including any new dwellings found during desktop canvassing. Field staff walked each geographic area attempting to identify missing dwellings and addresses unable to be found. The benefit of carrying out an in-person exercise is the ability for staff to contact residents for information not otherwise discernible. Staff members used tablet devices to access their workloads and record findings.
Maintaining independence from the census is necessary to allow PES to uncover dwellings that may have been missed by the census. The following measures were implemented across both the desktop canvassing and field enumeration to mitigate operational dependency:
- Staff employed to work on the PES could not be involved in subsequent work for the census. This applied to both field and office-based roles and extended to the recruitment agencies responsible for employing contractors.
- Access to PES data was restricted to staff working on PES.
- Field operations were run during periods of minimal census activity to limit the risk of overlap and accidental discovery of the PES sample areas by census staff.

#### 2.4.3 Sampling for interview
The next stage of determining the sample involved selecting eligible dwellings for interview from each PSU. Approximately 11 addresses were selected per PSU, resulting in around 16,500 addresses selected for interview. If multiple dwellings were located at the same address, all dwellings at that address were selected for interview (including any new dwellings at the address discovered during interview). Therefore, the number of dwellings selected for interview will be higher than the number of addresses.
Interviewers were provided with a list of dwellings selected for interview. They visited each dwelling with the aim of completing a household questionnaire for the dwelling and a personal questionnaire for each person within that dwelling. Interviews were conducted on the doorstep using a tablet device.
Revisiting the operational design outlined in the previous section, this section details how and when survey activities were implemented and discusses any notable deviations from the planned approach.
Implementation of the survey operation began with the field test, a trial run of the survey on a smaller sample, before verifying dwellings and conducting interviews on the full sample from June 2022 and June 2023, respectively. The timeline of work is outlined in figure 1, and the following sections provide details about each step.
Text alternative for Figure 1, Timeline of collection operations Diagram shows a timeline of 2023 PES collection operations. The field test phase began with desktop canvassing in November 2021, then field enumeration from November to December 2021. Interviews for the field test were carried out in 2022, from May to June. The dwelling verification phase began with desktop canvassing in June to September 2022, and finished with field enumeration in October to November 2022. The interview phase was carried out in 2023. It ran outside of the greater Auckland region from June to July, while it ran within the greater Auckland region July to August.
The field test was an opportunity to evaluate the approach for the enumeration and interview operations, including contingency plans for COVID-19 if safety measures limited staff from conducting face-to-face interviews. A sample of around 3,500 dwellings was selected in areas across Tauranga city, Whakatane district, Opotiki district, and Kawerau district. The operational areas for the test were selected to align with the 2022 Census field test (see 2022 field test report for the 2023 Census ).
In November 2021, office-based staff carried out desktop canvassing on a portion of the selected dwellings. The operation did not achieve the expected output volume or quality, where ‘poor quality’ equates to available information about a dwelling being missed. This was attributed to lack of dedicated staff resource, inefficiencies in managing workloads, and the need for more training on digital tools. These findings from the field test were addressed for the full survey operation.
After completing desktop canvassing, a field enumeration test was carried out from November to December 2021 on all dwellings in the sample. The start date was delayed because more time was needed for desktop canvassing and to address technical challenges with preparing and loading addresses into the collection tools used. Once in the field, staff made excellent progress and completed enumeration to a high quality in less time than expected.
From May to June 2022, an interview field test was carried out for around 500 dwellings in selected areas of Tauranga city, following the census field test in the same area from 14 February to 8 April 2022. Two interview methods were tested during this phase: residents at 300 dwellings were asked to participate in a face-to-face interview on the doorstep, while those at 200 dwellings were encouraged to respond using a telephone interview. Both options used the same questionnaire. The intent was to use tablets for conducting interviews as per the planned approach, but the required development was not completed in time. Instead, interviews for the field test were conducted on laptops in tablet mode, to mimic the final product.
The field test took place when COVID-19 safety measures were required. Despite the combination of mixed collection methods and the voluntary nature of participation, there was good participation from respondents. Successful responses were obtained from 192 dwellings, with nearly 400 people participating, and no major issues with the questionnaire were identified. The achieved sample rate (ASR) for dwellings was 38 percent, which is relatively high for a voluntary test. The response for telephone interviews was poorer than face-to-face interviews and improved only when dwellings were visited in person. Based on these findings, telephone interviews in this format were not considered viable for large-scale COVID-19 mitigation but could be an alternative for isolated cases.

#### 2.5.2 Dwelling verification
From June to September 2022, desktop canvassing staff used a geospatial tool, designed and developed based on learnings from the field test, to investigate addresses and interact with the available digital resources.
The initial sample frame consisted of 123,841 dwellings prior to desktop canvassing with the aim of enumerating 75 percent of these dwellings. Staff exceeded the target and enumerated 90 percent (110,871) and an additional 5,866 potential dwellings for further investigation in the field. The resulting dwelling frame used for field enumeration consisted of 129,707 dwellings.
From October to November 2022, 54 contractors carried out field enumeration, supported by six team leaders and around 40 Stats NZ field staff. During enumeration, an additional 9,100 dwellings were found and 7,000 dwellings were marked as not found. In total, 139,688 dwellings were enumerated with 131,984 dwellings confirmed in the field, making up 98.1 percent of the expected enumeration sample size.
Text alternative for Figure 2, Dwelling enumeration workflow Diagram shows the dwelling enumeration workflow. 123,841 addresses were selected in the initial sample frame, with 110,871 reviewed in desktop enumeration. 5,866 dwellings were added during desktop enumeration, resulting in a dwelling frame of 129,707 dwellings used for field enumeration. During field enumeration, 9,100 dwellings were added and 7,000 were not found, resulting in a total of 131,984 dwellings confirmed at the end of the dwelling enumeration process.
A dwelling may have been added because it was a genuine new dwelling, but it was often due to a change in dwelling usage or multiple dwellings previously being recorded as a single dwelling. A dwelling may have been removed because it was recently demolished, although other reasons, like changes in usage or on-site development with address changes, can also apply.

#### 2.5.3 Survey collection and interview
From June to August 2023, 151 contractors were recruited to carry out interviews at the 16,500 addresses selected for interview. At the conclusion of field operations, a total of 16,970 private dwellings were identified at these addresses. This includes cases where multiple dwellings were determined to be at one sample address, as well as additional dwellings that were identified and added during the interview process. In total, 14,047 dwellings completed the survey with over 37,600 people responding.
The 2023 Census collection was impacted by the lasting effects of the Auckland Anniversary weekend floods (27 January to 2 February 2023) and Cyclone Gabrielle (11 February to 17 February 2023). On 27 February 2023, Cabinet agreed to extend the census collection in response to the ongoing impacts caused by these weather events. Census collection was extended to 3 May 2023 across the country and to 1 June 2023 in heavily impacted areas.
These delays affected the starting date for PES interviews. To maintain the independence of PES from the census, PES interviews in an area could not begin until census activity in that area had concluded. As a result, dwellings located outside the greater Auckland region were interviewed from 2 June to 30 July 2023, and the remaining dwellings in the sample were interviewed from 1 July to 31 August 2023.
Modifications were also made to the sample to account for the extensive damage to housing and infrastructure in affected areas of the North Island. Dwellings that had been selected in the sample may no longer be habitable or accessible. To reduce the impact of sample loss, the sample size was increased in areas affected by flooding and cyclone damage. The scale of the increase in each PSU was determined based on the number of known damaged dwellings within that PSU.
Incentives were used during collection to mitigate the high risk of low response. It was anticipated that the 2023 PES would experience non-response rates similar to the 2023 Census. The 2023 PES took place between the 2023 Census and the 2023 Household Disability Survey, limiting the ability to address non-response using traditional methods, such as extending the field collection. At the time it was expected the response targets would not be met without the use of incentives.
A $30 grocery voucher was sent to around 10 percent of the total sample, targeted to one of two scenarios.
- Households in the Auckland region, located in areas with a New Zealand Index of Deprivation (NZDep) score of 8, 9, and 10, were sent a voucher with their survey notification letter (around 1,360 households).
- Outstanding households in cyclone-affected areas (Northland, Gisborne, Hawke's Bay) with an NZDep score of 5–10 were sent a voucher with a reminder letter (around 235 households). This excluded any households actively resisting or refusing to respond.
The 2023 PES achieved an overall response rate of 83.2 percent. When comparing similar households, incentivised households had an 87.5 percent response, while non-incentivised households had an 80.4 percent response.
The Post-Enumeration Survey (PES) estimates the coverage of the usually resident population of New Zealand who were present in the country on census night. It does this by sampling individuals in private dwellings and assuming that the coverage rates observed can be applied to residents in non-private dwellings as well.
Coverage refers to the ratio of the census usually resident population to the PES estimated usually resident population. This includes:
- net undercount – the difference between gross undercount and gross overcount, expressed as a number and percentage of the PES estimated population
- gross overcount – people counted more than once, or people incorrectly included in the census
- gross undercount – people missed by the census.
The 2023 PES uses a modified dual system estimation (DSE) approach, a capture-recapture method that links two datasets (census and PES) to identify individuals in both datasets, as well as those found in only one. For more detail, see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation .
The DSE approach is theoretically simple, but it relies on several key assumptions, including the following:
- Causal independence – assumes independency between census and PES inclusion. Dependence is minimised through operational separation, including separate staff, different address information, and field operations.
- Homogeneity of capture – assumes all records have the same likelihood of being captured in a given list. This is not achievable at a population level, so characteristics (such as age or gender) are used to group records until they have equal likelihood of capture.
- Perfect linking between two datasets – assumes error-free linking between the census and PES records including no missed links, and no records incorrectly linked.
- No erroneous inclusion – means exclusion of non-target population records (for example, births after, deaths before, or residents overseas on census night).
- Closed population – assumes no changes (entries or exits) between data captures. In addition to running the PES soon after the census to minimise changes in population, we approximate it nationally using birth, death, and border data, while at a subnational level, we assume minimal internal migration impact.
More information on these assumptions and DSE is provided in 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation . The processes outlined in this chapter are designed to ensure the assumptions are met.
To meet these assumptions and successfully achieve the PES's objectives, we followed a comprehensive design outlined in the standard design framework and PES adaptive design. However, during implementation, we encountered challenges that required careful consideration and adjustments. This chapter provides a brief overview of the key technical aspects of PES implementation, highlighting any modifications or additional measures taken to refine and enhance the standard design.
Following the processing of PES response data, it is linked to the 2023 Census dataset to identify which PES records are representative of undercount and overcount. For us to confidently identify census coverage error, we need to achieve ‘perfect linking’ – that is, every PES record is linked to every census record referring to the same person. This is a critical requirement for the use of our estimation system (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation ).
Linkage error comes in two forms:
- false positive error – two records are incorrectly linked
- false negative error – two records that refer to the same person are not linked.
There are no unique identifiers for residents in New Zealand so the PES and census datasets cannot be directly matched. Instead, the linking process between these two sources is achieved by probabilistic linking – comparing information about an individual, such as full name, date of birth, and gender, and linking the records that belong to the same individual. This process comes with potential linkage error, for example, caused by incomplete or inconsistent data between sources.
With probabilistic linking, zero linkage error cannot be achieved as some records will be incorrectly matched, whereas links between others will be missed. However, the rates of both false positive and false negative linkage error need to be low enough that any remaining linkage error would have a negligible impact on the coverage estimates. The following quality targets were determined for 2023 PES and were measured through a quality assurance sampling process (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Linking design ):
- less than 0.5 percent of PES records with one link are assessed as a false positive link
- less than 0.5 percent of PES records with more than one link are assessed as having at least one false positive link
- less than 0.5 percent of PES records with no link are assessed as a false negative link
- less than 0.5 percent of PES records with one link are assessed as having a false negative link to an additional record.
The above targets were set at levels that are practically achievable. While 0.5 percent linkage error is not negligible, the intention was that by having a tangible goal, the linking processes would be robust and followed by a comprehensive quality assurance process to ensure the 0.5 percent targets were met. By having balanced false negative and false positive linkage error thresholds, the intention is that the net error will be close to 0 percent for the overall population. All these targets were met in the 2023 PES-to-census linking process. More details on the linking process are available in 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Final linking methodology .

### 3.2 Ethnicity groupings used for coverage estimation
A key assumption for our coverage estimation process is homogeneity of capture – that all individuals have the same likelihood of being captured in a given list. In other words, all individuals should have the same chance as each other of being captured in PES, or all individuals in the census have the same chance of being counted as each other. This assumption is not always true for the entire population, but the risk of bias can be mitigated by grouping people with similar characteristics like age, gender, ethnicity, and geographic location until the probability of being counted is the same for all.
The original design for the 2023 coverage estimation (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation ) assumed that level 1 ethnicity groupings ( Ethnicity New Zealand Standard Classification 2005 V2.1.0 ) used by Stats NZ would create homogeneous groupings when disaggregated with other demographic variables such as age, gender, and geographic information. However, during the PES-to-census linking process it was found that records for people in the Chinese and Indian ethnic groups were prone to different forms of linkage error. Records for people of the Chinese ethnic group tended to be more prone to false negative linkage error (missed links), while records for people of the Indian ethnic group were more often incorrectly linked (false positive error). This meant that we could no longer assume homogeneity of capture in the Asian ethnic group.
This difference in linkage error had a strong correlation with Chinese name structures and the high frequency of some Indian surnames. We used the Chinese and Indian level 2 ethnic groups as a proxy for the differences in linkage error caused by these differing name structures and naming conventions, as this ethnic information was available within the survey data. An alternative would have been to create a ’name structure’ variable but this would have required additional research and development and was not within scope.  For this reason, the estimation design was updated to treat Chinese and Indian as two separate ethnic groups, rather than combining them into a single Asian ethnic group. However, the level 1 ethnicity grouping Asian was also used as part of estimation, in addition to these two indicators (so, for example, a record for a person in the Chinese ethnic group would be classified as both Asian and Chinese).

### 3.3 Treatment of missing data
The PES estimation framework requires all key demographic variables to be complete to accurately adjust the count for different population groups. These variables include ethnicity, Māori descent, age, gender, a New Zealand-born indicator, and usual residence location (at the territorial authority/local board (TALB) level).
The 2023 PES imputation design outlines two main phases to treating missing information: deterministic imputation and statistical imputation (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation ).
Although the 2023 PES approach for treating missing data is based on the 2018 design, it has been updated to include more alternative data sources in the deterministic imputation phase. This builds on the extensive use of alternative data sources in the 2018 Census and improves the 2023 PES imputation design by reducing the amount of imputation required in the statistical imputation phase.
In the deterministic imputation phase, the missing value is filled by copying information from alternative data sources in a linked dataset. The order of using alternative data sources is generally as follows, with some variation between the order of c) and d) depending on the variable:
- 2023 Census values sourced from individual form responses
- 2023 Census values sourced from dwelling forms or household set-up forms
- Historical census data
- 2023 Census values sourced through statistical imputation.
This varied from the standard design for deterministic imputation, where all information from a census record (including the use of alternative data sources and statistical imputation) was prioritised over alternative data sources sourced through the link made between PES and administrative data (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Linking design , 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation ). Analysis was carried out on the impact of this change and found to have a negligible effect on the variables used in estimation.
Following this deterministic imputation, in the statistical imputation phase the missing value is filled by borrowing information from similar records (donors) in the PES dataset, through a random selection from a pool of similar donor records.
There is a small difference for imputing the gender variable. If a PES response has a missing gender value, it was deterministically imputed using 2023 Census responses. Where a value was not available, PES statistical imputation was applied. Additional administrative sources were not used due to their limited gender information and the frequent conflation of gender with sex at birth, often collected without clear distinctions. Moreover, most administrative sources record this information as a binary variable, which could introduce bias and underrepresent individuals identifying outside the binary categories (see Methodologies for filling gaps in gender and sex at birth concepts for the 2023 Census ). To avoid this, any remaining missing gender values were imputed through the PES statistical imputation process, consistent with the approach used in other household surveys.
We followed the 2023 PES standard design for gender imputation and didn't see any indication which would require a change of methodology to fit with the 2023 Census design, which included Ministry of Social Development records from December 2019 onwards (see Methodologies for filling gaps in gender and sex at birth concepts for the 2023 Census ).
When considering a te ao Māori perspective, we have been cognisant of the proposed imputation of ethnicity and Māori descent information, as well as the use of administrative data sources. During the design process we have consulted with internal advisors within Stats NZ to ensure the methods and data sources proposed are appropriate and culturally acceptable. An analysis of the differences in reporting between PES and census is outlined in Differences in reporting .

### 3.4 Treatment of over-coverage caused by duplicate census records in PES estimation
When a PES response is linked to multiple census records, it is necessary to classify one of these records as being representative of ‘coverage’, and the rest as ‘over-coverage’. Ensuring a consistent treatment to determine which record is noted as over-coverage improves the accuracy and reliability of the PES estimation process.
Following the 2023 PES linking process, it was found that when a PES record had linked to multiple census responses (indicating over-coverage), there was a strong correlation between the data source of the census record (that is, an individual form response, a response that is only from a paper dwelling form or online household set-up form, or an admin enumeration (see Census individual unit record data source indicator V3.0.0 )) and whether they appeared as part of a census duplicate pair. This relationship was used to identify which records were prioritised as being representative of ‘coverage’.
Two groups of census data sources were over-represented in the detected census duplicate pairs (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Final linking methodology ). The first of these groups were meshblock admin enumerations. These are admin enumerations that do not meet the criteria to be enumerated into a dwelling. However, census processes indicate they are usual residents of New Zealand and live in a certain meshblock (see Methodology for using admin data to count people in the 2023 Census ).  The second group were records that only appear on paper dwelling forms or online household set-up forms. Meanwhile, census individual form responses were under-represented. Consequently, in a duplicate pair containing an individual form and a meshblock admin enumeration, the individual form would always be chosen as representative of ‘coverage’ while the meshblock admin enumeration was selected as representative of ‘over-coverage’.
The eventual ranking used to determine which record was treated as representative of ‘coverage’ was:
- individual census responses
- whole household admin enumerations
- admin-linked absentees
- within-household admin enumerations
- responses from online household set-up or paper dwelling forms
- meshblock admin enumerations.
Through the implementation phase we found that records from paper dwelling forms and online household set-up forms were highly over-represented in the duplicates. This indicated that they were a strong driver of over-coverage in the census (either due to a lack of linkage to individual forms or because they were not identified as part of the admin ever-resident population of New Zealand). Based on this we moved them down the ranking, below admin enumerations that had enough information to be assigned to a dwelling.
Both the coverage record and the over-coverage record go into the over-coverage model. As long as the attributes of these records are the same, there shouldn't be an impact on the results caused by which is selected as over-coverage. Using data source as an over-coverage predictor could have further refined the over-coverage patterns, but this was found to be unnecessary.

### 3.5 Treatment of the 2023 Census file for use in coverage estimation
Following both linking and treatment of missing data, the set of complete PES records undergoes a series of derivations to create all the information needed for estimation processes. This is followed by to the estimation process itself (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation ).
The key outputs from estimation listed below are produced for all population groups of interest:
- PES estimated population
- estimated net undercount achieved by census
- estimated gross overcount in the census dataset
- estimated gross undercount in the census dataset.

#### 3.5.1 Various estimation concepts
Estimation derivations and management of how different records are treated throughout estimation are critical to ensuring the robustness of the dual system estimation assumptions. This relates to how the census file is created, and how different types of records in PES and the census are incorporated into the estimation process. Key parts of the PES and census components are explained below. More details about these components and the relevant design decisions are available in 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation . Any deviations from the standard design are detailed below.

#### 3.5.2 Census eligible population
The census eligible population is the high-level group of census records that are eligible for coverage estimation. The approach for deriving this group for the 2023 PES coverage estimation was mostly aligned with the method developed for the 2018 PES.
For this, the 2023 Census dataset was firstly subset to the group of records for usual residents of New Zealand who were in the country on census night (record types 3 and 4 in Census Individual Record Type V3.0.0 ). This dataset was then segmented based on the following factors to derive the population eligible for coverage estimation:
- quality of linking information – records in the census file were split into those with high-quality linking information and those without (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Linking design for the specific criteria used). Records with low-quality linking information were also removed from PES.
- type of record – high-quality records in the census file were split into those that come from a census form response (both individual form responses and those who only appear on a paper dwelling form or online household set-up form), and those that are admin enumerations. Both census responses and admin enumerations include individuals that usually reside in non-private dwellings. PES does not interview people in non-private dwellings. With the exception of admin enumerations in prisons, these individuals are assumed to have the same coverage patterns as those residing in private dwellings and are therefore not treated differently for coverage estimation and census count adjustment (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation ).
- Both census responses and admin enumerations include individuals that usually reside in non-private dwellings. PES does not interview people in non-private dwellings. With the exception of admin enumerations in prisons, these individuals are assumed to have the same coverage patterns as those residing in private dwellings and are therefore not treated differently for coverage estimation and census count adjustment (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation ).
- presence in New Zealand on census night – high-quality census form responses and admin enumerations are split into those who were in New Zealand on census night, and those identified from admin data as having been overseas on census night (residents temporarily overseas, or RTOs). More information on how RTO status was derived is available in Residents temporarily overseas (RTOs) .
- time of response – high-quality census form responses for people present in New Zealand on census night were split into those received before the start of PES interviewing and those received after (late). This was adapted to account for the extended collection period in Auckland in 2023 (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Adaptive design for coverage estimation ), as explained in Census late responses .
- type of admin enumeration – admin enumerations in prisons were separated from the remaining admin enumerations (mostly in private dwellings or at meshblock level). More information on why admin enumerations in prisons were excluded from coverage estimation is available below and in 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation .
Figure 3 shows this segmentation in visual form. The blue boxes reflect the census records that were eligible for inclusion in coverage estimation. The yellow boxes show the census records that were mostly excluded from coverage estimation. Records in the red box were treated as under-coverage and accounted for in the gross under-coverage calculation (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation ).

As part of the 2023 Census process, admin enumerations were added into prisons, based on manifests provided by the Department of Corrections. These individuals were highly unlikely to be part of the PES survey population, which excludes people living in non-private dwellings (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation ). Analysis confirmed that this assumption remained valid in the 2023 PES design. In addition, the use of agency-supplied records means we were confident the census counts for prison populations were reliable and did not share the same coverage patterns as the rest of the population.
Therefore, these admin enumerations in non-private dwellings were excluded from the PES estimation process. They are included in the total population estimation but are assumed to have no coverage error.
While the standard design accounted for census records enumerated into defence facilities as well as in prisons, the 2023 Census ended up not using information from the New Zealand Defence Force (NZDF) to add individuals into the census dataset (see Methodology for using admin data to count people in the 2023 Census ). Instead, NZDF personnel who did not respond to the census were eligible to be added into the file through the normal admin enumeration process (into private or non-private dwellings, or as a meshblock enumeration). Therefore, they were eligible for coverage estimation, unlike those enumerated into prisons.
A resident temporarily overseas (RTO) is a person who usually lives in New Zealand but was overseas on census night. The 2023 Census used border crossing data to identify census responses from RTOs and to exclude them from the census usually resident and census night population counts, removing them as a source of over-coverage. This reduced the amount of over-coverage caused by RTOs compared with what was seen in 2018.
The original (standard design) for PES would not have used border crossing data to exclude additional records from the 2023 Census file. However, the final design used in 2023 PES identified RTOs in the 2023 Census file by using the border crossing data used to derive Stats NZ’s official migration statistics. These records were then excluded from coverage estimation, resulting in 5,589 records being removed from the census usually resident population (CURP).
Analysis was carried out to understand the differences between border crossing data used to derive CURP and that used to detect RTOs in the census dataset as part of the 2023 PES process. Due to the high quality of the entity resolution (working out if two records refer to the same person) and data cleaning (removal of erroneous border crossing movements) carried out within the border crossing data used by the 2023 PES process, we are confident that these 5,589 records were truly overseas on census night 2023 and are therefore true over-coverage.
These 5,589 records are treated as over-coverage in our calculation of census KPIs, and they would have been treated as RTOs (that is, excluded) from the PES estimated population counts. However, they were added back into the population for the ERP at census night.
As in the standard design, PES records were removed from the coverage estimation process if border crossing information or the person indicated during the interview that they were overseas on census night (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation ). However, the border crossing data used to determine RTO status for PES records was that used in the 2023 PES process to identify census RTOs.
A census late response is a census individual form completed and received on or after the start date of PES field activities. If census responses are received late, there is a possibility that the awareness of PES occurring in the field could influence a PES respondent to complete a census form (when they had not already). This means that the PES process may inadvertently impact the census returns. To avoid violating the causal independence assumption, the PES methodology treats on-time and late census responses separately. To handle census late responses in PES estimation, late responses are temporarily removed from the estimation model and then added back in after estimation – a method called ‘remove and add’ (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation ).
The start date of the PES was postponed in Auckland due to the census outreach activities of the Whānau Ora Commission Agency (formerly Te Pou Matakana), which aimed to increase response rates among Māori and Pacific households in Auckland. To account for this split collection period, two different classifications were used for late responses based on the usual residence address recorded in the census. A response was classified as ‘late’ if it was received:
- after 1 June 2023 in all geographic areas outside the Auckland local boards
- after 30 June 2023 in Auckland local boards.
The 2023 PES adaptive design investigated how the different methods of accounting for this split collection period could affect the statistical assumptions used in coverage estimation (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Adaptive design for coverage estimation ). The findings showed that:
- late responses are unlikely to be influenced by or prevented by PES activity
- late responses exhibit a correlation with under-coverage
- the PES sample contained proportionally fewer late responses than the census for specific groups, such as young males and individuals of Pacific ethnicity.
The standard design approach of removing census late responses from coverage estimation before adding them back into the final population estimates was found to be the most appropriate way of dealing with the split collection, as long as the 2023 PES sampling of late responses was not clustered. As the adaptive design analysis was carried out on 2018 data, to ensure that the findings held up with 2023 data, a sensitivity analysis was carried out to ensure that ‘remove and add’ was still the appropriate method. This analysis was carried out by running a simplified version of the full 2023 estimation model (see Estimation methods ) on the PES data with late responses treated in three ways:
- remove and add – PES records linked to late responses were removed from estimation and late response counts were added in after under- and over-coverage correction
- treat as coverage – late responses were treated the same way as all other responses
- treat as under-coverage – late responses were removed from the census file, and where a PES record was linked to a late response, this PES record was treated as under-coverage.
The model produced similar results for each method indicating a low sensitivity to the treatment of late responses. As such, the planned method, ‘remove and add’, was the method used.

### 3.6 Differences in reporting
When both PES and the census collect the same variable (for example, ethnicity, gender), discrepancies between their reported values may occur, known as a difference in reporting. This section examines these differences for some key estimation variables used in estimation.
To be included in the analysis, the values for the variable of interest should come directly from PES and census responses. Records where that specific variable was imputed in either PES or census are excluded. However, if a different variable in the record was imputed but the variable of interest was not, the record remains in the analysis. The PES data used in this analysis were unweighted survey data.
While most people are expected to provide the same responses in both the PES and the census –since both use the same standard protocols, ask the same questions in the same way, and people respond to both PES and the census in a relatively small window of time – some natural variation in reporting may occur. Additionally, differences could arise from the distinct collection methods: PES interviews are typically conducted face-to-face or by phone, while the census is a self-completed survey, primarily completed online or via paper forms.
We investigate differences in reporting for ethnicity, Māori descent, gender, and usual residence location. Comparing PES and census responses for these variables provide valuable insight into response variations. These differences were analysed to ensure that the inconsistencies between the responses are not remarkable and do not introduce any biases to the estimation process.
Results in this section show that for the variables considered in the analysis, there were no systematic patterns in the differences in reporting between the PES and census and they can be considered as natural variations. This means that there is no need to make any adjustments to the estimation process. It is important to note that published PES results are based on the attributes recorded by the PES, even if they are different to the recorded census attributes for a person, as only the PES has the attributes for people missed by census.
The analysis uses level 1 ethnicity groupings based on the Ethnicity New Zealand Standard Classification 2005 V2.1.0 .  For each ethnicity, people are divided into ‘yes’ and ‘no’ groups. If a person identifies with an ethnicity, they are counted in the ‘yes’ group; if not, they are in the ‘no’ group. For example, the ‘yes’ group for Māori includes people who reported their ethnicity as Māori only or reported multiple ethnic groups including Māori.

#### 3.6.4 Usual residence location
Differences in reporting of usual residence location were investigated at two geographic levels for which coverage estimates are produced: regional council and territorial authority and Auckland local board (TALB).
The findings indicate that 97.0 percent of respondents provided consistent responses for their usual residence at the TALB level in both the PES and the census. Some detailed findings are:
- 1,002 PES respondents whose TALB of usual residence in PES differed from the TALB of their usual residence in the census.
- Movement to and from TALBs shows similar patterns. The number of people moving from a TALB has a maximum of 42. Movement to a TALB has a maximum of 45 people. These counts are based on PES respondents whose TALB of usual residence differed between the census and PES. As numbers are so small, percentages are not meaningful.
- TALBs with the highest in-movement and out-movements were all in the Auckland region.
Additionally, 98.9 percent of respondents reported consistent usual residence at the regional council level across the PES and census. Some detailed findings are:
- 378 PES respondents whose regional council of usual residence in PES differed from their regional council of usual residence in the census.
- Movement between regional councils was similar in both directions. Movement from a regional council has a maximum of 99 people. Movement to a regional council has a maximum of 78 people. These numbers reflect PES respondents who reported a different regional council in the census and PES.
- All regional councils with the highest in-movement and out-movement were in the Auckland region.
Findings show no systematic patterns in the differences in reported TALB and regional council of usual residence. This is supported by having similar median of movement in and out of TALBs or regional councils.

### 3.7 Deriving required variables for coverage estimation
The derivation step determines which PES records are to be included in estimation, using scope and eligibility information of the records.  Derived variables indicate if the PES respondents were correctly included into the census file. This is done via the creation of SHOULD and WAS indicators.
- SHOULD: Indicates whether a PES respondent should have been counted in the census. 1 = If a PES respondent should have been counted in the census 0 = If a PES respondent should not have been counted in the census.
- 1 = If a PES respondent should have been counted in the census
- 0 = If a PES respondent should not have been counted in the census.
- WAS: Reflects the number of times a respondent was counted in the census. 1 = if a PES respondent WAS counted ONCE in the census 0 = if a PES respondent WAS NOT counted in the census.
- 1 = if a PES respondent WAS counted ONCE in the census
- 0 = if a PES respondent WAS NOT counted in the census.
In practice, these derivations are further refined to account for PES scope and eligibility concepts, the estimation approach, and the treatment of different types of census records (such as RTOs, late returns, and admin enumerations). Although most aspects of the derivation phase were implemented following the PES standard design, we made some adjustments to two areas: 1) treatment of over-coverage caused by duplicate census records in PES estimation, and 2) ethnicity groupings used for coverage estimation. Both items were discussed earlier in this chapter.
The derivation of SHOULD and WAS variables are then used for identifying under-coverage (individuals who should have been counted but were not) and over-coverage (individuals who should not have been counted or were counted more than once).
- Under01 (Under-coverage indicator): 1 = Respondent should have been counted but was not 0 = Respondent should have been counted and was counted
- 1 = Respondent should have been counted but was not 0 = Respondent should have been counted and was counted
- 1 = Respondent should have been counted but was not
- 0 = Respondent should have been counted and was counted
- Over01 (Over-coverage indicator): 1 = Either: Respondent should have been counted once but was counted more than once, or Respondent should not have been counted but was counted 0 = Respondent should have been counted once and was counted once
- 1 = Either: Respondent should have been counted once but was counted more than once, or Respondent should not have been counted but was counted
- Respondent should have been counted once but was counted more than once, or
- Respondent should not have been counted but was counted
- 0 = Respondent should have been counted once and was counted once
The derived Under01 and Over01 variables are then included in the estimation process alongside the demographic and geographic attributes.

### 3.8 Estimation methods
The 2023 PES, like the 2018 PES, uses a Bayesian modelling approach. This method is a modification of the popular dual-system estimation (DSE) method. In this approach, two Bayesian multilevel generalised linear models were fitted to under- and over-coverage datasets. Under- and over-coverage datasets were generated following the derivation step and include all the necessary information for the estimation process.
The proposed models use binary indicators for under- and over-coverage, Under01 and Over01, regressed against demographic and geographic covariates. The under-coverage model had a similar structure to the model used in 2018 PES (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation , Appendix 10). Similar model fitting and checking procedures used in 2018 were also followed. The 2018 model and associated fitting and checking procedures are described in Small Domain Estimation of Census Coverage – A Case Study in Bayesian Analysis of Complex Survey Data .
The 2018 PES over-coverage model was a simplified model compared to the under-coverage one (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation , Appendix 10). This was sufficient because of the small number of over-coverage records. In 2023, we observed an increase in over-coverage records (see 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Final linking methodology ) as well as higher variability of over-coverage proportions between different demographic groups. As a result, we required a more complex over-coverage model and found the under-coverage model structure was appropriate.
The following changes were made to the individual level covariates used in the 2023 models, compared with those used in 2018:
- Addition of covariates for MELAA and Other ethnicities to better reflect coverage patterns in these groups.
- Addition of covariates for Chinese and Indian ethnicities, due to the observed linking rates as explained in subsection Ethnicity groupings used for coverage estimation . This was in addition to a more general indicator for Asian ethnicity. The encoding for these ethnicities was done as independent binary indicators, consistent with the approach for level 1 ethnicities.
- Use of a three-category gender variable instead of a two-category sex at birth variable. This change reflects the shift in the 2023 PES to collect gender instead of sex at birth, following the inclusion of gender as a concept in the 2023 Census (see Methodologies for filling gaps in gender and sex at birth concepts for the 2023 Census ) and in accordance with the 2022 Data standard for gender, sex, and variations of sex characteristics .
- Use of a binary indicator for usual residence meshblocks affected by Cyclone Gabrielle.
The models were used to produce posterior distributions for under-coverage and over-coverage probabilities for demographic and geographic groups of interest. These estimated under-coverage and over-coverage probabilities are used to calculate coverage adjustment ratios The adjusted population counts were calculated using corresponding coverage adjustment ratios applied to eligible census counts (see Census eligible population ). The final PES estimated population includes the coverage-adjusted census population, late census responses, and administrative enumerations in prisons.
National total is calculated as a sum of all demographic subgroups:
x,t – demographic and geographic covariates defining a sub-population
census elig – census count of high-quality admin enumerations, and on-time census form responses for residents present in New Zealand on census night (irrespective of responding in private dwelling or non-private dwelling)
R – coverage adjustment ratio produced from the combined under- and over-coverage models
census late – census count of late returns with high-quality linking information
census NPD – census count of prison admin enumerations
Using the PES estimated population, we can then produce the net undercount as the difference between the PES estimated population and the census usual resident population count.

#### 3.8.1 Estimated gross undercount and overcount of the census usual resident population
Gross undercount is estimated using the under-coverage model operating on the census eligible records (see Census eligible population ). The estimate is then adjusted to account for records excluded from the census eligible population due to poor quality of linking information.
census elig = census count of high-quality admin enumerations (excluding those in prison establishments), and on-time census form responses for residents present in New Zealand on census night (irrespective of responding in private dwelling or non-private dwelling)
P ucov = the estimated under-coverage probabilities for the census elig records
P ocov = the estimated over-coverage probabilities for the census elig records
census linkrej = census count of records with poor-quality linking information (includes on-time returns, late returns, and non-prison admin enumerations).
Gross overcount considers people counted more than once or who should not have been counted. This is produced by combining the over-coverage probabilities of the census eligible records and over-coverage directly observed from RTOs.
Gross overcount(x,t) = census elig (x,t) × p ocov (x,t) + census RTO (x,t)
census elig = census count of high-quality admin enumerations (excluding those in prison establishments), and on-time census form responses for residents present in New Zealand on census night (irrespective of responding in private dwelling or non-private dwelling)
p ocov = the estimated over-coverage probabilities for the census elig records
census RTO = census count of records identified as RTOs using admin data on border movements ( Residents_temporarily_overseas ).
More detailed descriptions of the models and calculation of the coverage measures can be found in 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Standard design for coverage estimation .

#### 3.8.2 Credible intervals
As in the 2018 PES, the uncertainty of the 2023 PES outputs is reflected in credible intervals. Credible intervals provide a probability-based range within which the true value is likely to fall. For example, a 95 percent credible interval means there is a 95 percent chance that the true value lies within that range.
Credible intervals are derived from the posterior distributions, which represent the probability distributions of the estimated quantities. In PES outputs, 95 percent credible intervals are defined by the 2.5 percent and 97.5 percent quantiles of the posterior distributions.
While credible intervals report the uncertainty tied to sample-based estimates, they do not provide a comprehensive picture of non-sampling errors in PES (for example, imputation uncertainty, uncertainty around statistical design assumptions, and linking error).
An overview other potential sources of uncertainty in the 2023 PES is provided in 2023 Post-enumeration Survey: Adaptive design for coverage estimation .

### 4.1 Key performance indicators (KPIs)

#### 4.1.1 2023 Census KPIs
A set of key performance indicators (KPIs) was established to measure how successfully the 2023 Census was delivered. The 2023 PES is responsible for measuring some of these KPIs – specifically, the coverage and response rates at the national level and for people of Māori descent, as well as coverage rates for Pacific peoples.
Coverage rates measure the completeness of the census count of the New Zealand population (including subnational populations). They include people counted through 2023 Census forms as well as through administrative data collected by other agencies (admin enumerations).
Responses rates measure the percentage of the population living in New Zealand who were counted on census forms. These are lower than census coverage rates as they exclude those added through admin enumerations.
2023 Census KPI results are available from Measuring the success of the 2023 Census .
In addition to measuring coverage and response rates for a set of 2023 Census KPIs, the 2023 PES project also had its own targets. These KPIs focused on PES survey response, sample frame completeness, and the precision of published coverage estimates. They were designed to assure the quality of the 2023 PES outputs, from survey design and data collection through to dissemination.
The 2023 PES KPIs were developed with input from key customers. While they are broadly similar to those used in the 2018 PES, several notable changes were introduced to incorporate improvements identified following the 2018 PES cycle and to meet emerging customer needs:
- a regional achieved sample rate (ASR) target was introduced, alongside the existing national ASR target, to reduce response rate variability between regional council areas
- a new target for the dwelling-enumeration phase, focusing on the completeness (or accuracy) of the dwelling sample frame that was used to select the addresses for interview
- new precision targets for coverage estimates of male and female genders, people of Māori descent (electoral) , and lower-level geographies (including territorial authority and Auckland local board areas). No precision target was set for people of another gender – the sample size for this group was expected to be small, leading to large uncertainties in the coverage estimates that could be difficult to quantify before data collection.
A total of 11 KPI categories were set for the 2023 PES. The KPI target was achieved in eight categories and not met in the other three, as shown in table 5. These targets were designed to be challenging to achieve, and in some cases, aspirational.
Of the three unmet KPIs, two were partially met (subnational ASR and ethnic group precision), and one fell only slightly below the target (national PES response rate).

The achieved sample rate (ASR) is a proxy measure for survey response rates, and provides a measure of household response while PES collection is ongoing. It counts the number of raw (unweighted) households responding to the survey as a proportion of all dwellings sampled, before each household’s eligibility status has been determined.
Monitoring the ASR throughout survey collection enables action to be taken quickly if rates are tracking lower than expected.
The survey response rates are calculated following survey completion, once the eligibility status of each household has been determined. Compared to the ASR, it counts the weighted number of eligible responding households as a proportion of all eligible households. For households of unknown eligibility, this is estimated using the proportion of known eligibility households.
